content:
Ryan, I’d use Psalm 19 to disciple my child in how genre works without ever weakening truth.

- During (or right after) the reading I’d quietly say: “This is a poem. Poems use pictures—‘the sun in a tent,’ ‘the heavens declare’—but the things it says are 100% true: God made the world and His word is perfect.” Then I’d point to verses 1–6 (creation speaks) and 7–11 (God’s law speaks) and say, “Both are God’s truth.”

- I’d connect it to Genesis 1: “Some adults say Genesis 1 is ‘poetic’ or ‘artful.’ Even if God told the story in a beautiful, patterned way, it still tells what really happened: God created everything by His word, made people in His image, made them male and female, called creation very good, ordered it with purpose, and set a rhythm of work and rest.” I’d make clear that poetry never means “not true”; it means “true things said with pictures.”

- If my child asks about the “days,” I’d say, “Christians who love the Bible have differed on whether those days are 24-hour days or a literary frame. What we don’t differ on is that God actually created and that Adam and Eve were real and the fall was real. We hold to what Scripture clearly affirms.”

- With the elder, I’d thank him for seeking unity, then gently suggest a better path: “Let’s guard unity by affirming inerrancy and the core creation truths, while allowing some room on the length of the days. But let’s not call Genesis ‘poetry’ in a way that makes people think it’s less than history, just as we don’t treat Psalm 19’s claims about God’s law as ‘only poetry.’” I’d ask that we teach our people how genre works rather than using genre to downgrade meaning.

Practically, next Sunday I’d have my 10‑year‑old memorize Psalm 19:1 and 19:7 with me, then read Genesis 1 together that afternoon, pointing out the repeated phrases. I’d finish with, “God’s world is true. God’s word is true. And God tells His truth in many beautiful ways.”
